hypothesis: Per-model sliding-window median should beat global median on BurstGPT HF because ChatGPT (median=7) and GPT-4 (median=212) have 30x different output lengths, so global median≈14 misclassifies all GPT-4 requests as short.
research_basis: EWSJF (arXiv:2601.21758, KDD 2026): per-group SJF achieves 30%+ throughput gain
Entropy-Guided Output Length Prediction (arXiv:2602.11812, ICLR 2026): p95/p50 ratios 1.7-20.5 across models
BurstGPT (arXiv:2401.17644, KDD 2025): ChatGPT vs GPT-4 heterogeneity
conclusion: NULL RESULT. Model stratification provides only +0.12% gain over global prior (72.85% vs 72.76% oracle retention). Root cause: within-model output-length variability remains extremely high (CV≈94% for both global and stratified). ChatGPT has heavy tail (median=7, mean=103.9); GPT-4 is broadly variable (median=235, mean=253.8). Per-model median is a better expected-value estimator but does not improve SRPT ordering because residual variability within each model dominates.
verdict: CLOSED. Model-id stratification does not close the oracle gap on BurstGPT HF. Need request-specific features (context length, prompt structure, task type) to reduce within-model CV below 50%.
next_best_opportunity: CARA HGB request-specific output-token predictor using context-length + request-type features. Historical CV on Azure: 7% for low-context-length bin. If applicable, would close 18pp Azure gap and potentially 12pp+ BurstGPT gap.
